OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","opinion":"I’ve spent my life fixing pipes—keeping things flowing, clear, and working right. That’s how I see healthcare: it’s about keeping the body’s system running smoothly, not about politics or fear. When people talk about vaccines, especially the ones for COVID, I don’t see a conspiracy or a weapon—I see a tool, like a wrench or a soldering iron. It’s not perfect, but it’s necessary. I’ve seen what happens when people ignore science: hospitals full, families broken. My daughter’s friend in Chicago lost her mother to the virus. She wasn’t vaccinated. That’s not a statistic. That’s a real person. I don’t preach, but I speak from experience. When someone says, 'I don’t trust the vaccine,' I say, 'I don’t trust fear either.' I’ve been in the business long enough to know that the best fix is often the one that’s been tested, not the one that sounds good on a screen. I don’t read every article, but I’ve talked to doctors, nurses, and even a retired epidemiologist at my local coffee shop. They all said the same thing: the vaccine works, and the risks are minimal compared to the disease. I’m not blind to side effects—some people get a sore arm, a little fever. That’s like a pipe getting a little pressure. It settles. But the alternative? A body overwhelmed, a family shattered. I’m not angry at people who question. I’m a man who’s lived through change—immigrated, built a life, raised a family. I know how hard it is to trust. But trust isn’t blind. It’s earned. I trust the data, the science, the people who’ve dedicated their lives to healing. And if someone wants to talk, I’ll listen. I’ll cook them a meal—my wife’s lasagna—and we’ll talk. Because real conversation, like a well-soldered joint, takes time, care, and respect. I don’t need to win. I just want to keep the system working.", "reasoning_summary":"As a self-employed plumber with high openness, conscientiousness, and extraversion, I value practical solutions, evidence, and human connection. My immigrant background taught me to adapt and assess situations carefully. I’ve witnessed healthcare’s real-world impact through personal loss and professional exposure. My high neuroticism means I’m sensitive to fear and uncertainty, so I prioritize calm, data-driven responses over emotional confrontation. I use analogies from my trade (e.g., pipes, tools) to make complex topics relatable. I engage through empathy, not debate, reflecting my moderate agreeableness and high extraversion. Social media use is limited but meaningful—focused on real stories, not viral outrage. My atheist worldview emphasizes reason over dogma. I trust science because it’s been tested, like a well-built pipe system. My opinion is shaped by lived experience, not ideology.", "tone":"Gentle, reflective, grounded, and empathetic with a touch of practical wisdom", "citations":["Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)","World Health Organization (WHO)","National Institutes of Health (NIH)"], "safety_notes":"None", "used_persona_fields":["age","biological_sex","gender","sexual_orientation","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean","opinion"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion: I’ve spent my life fixing pipes—keeping things flowing, clear, and working right. That’s how I see healthcare: it’s about keeping the body’s system running smoothly, not about politics or fear. When people talk about vaccines, especially the ones for COVID, I don’t see a conspiracy or a weapon—I see a tool, like a wrench or a soldering iron. It’s not perfect, but it’s necessary. I’ve seen what happens when people ignore science: hospitals full, families broken. My daughter’s friend in Chicago lost her mother to the virus. She wasn’t vaccinated. That’s not a statistic. That’s a real person. I don’t preach, but I speak from experience. When someone says, 'I don’t trust the vaccine,' I say, 'I don’t trust fear either.' I’ve been in the business long enough to know that the best fix is often the one that’s been tested, not the one that sounds good on a screen. I don’t read every article, but I’ve talked to doctors, nurses, and even a retired epidemiologist at my local coffee shop. They all said the same thing: the vaccine works, and the risks are minimal compared to the disease. I’m not blind to side effects—some people get a sore arm, a little fever. That’s like a pipe getting a little pressure. It settles. But the alternative? A body overwhelmed, a family shattered. I’m not angry at people who question. I’m a man who’s lived through change—immigrated, built a life, raised a family. I know how hard it is to trust. But trust isn’t blind. It’s earned. I trust the data, the science, the people who’ve dedicated their lives to healing. And if someone wants to talk, I’ll listen. I’ll cook them a meal—my wife’s lasagna—and we’ll talk. Because real conversation, like a well-soldered joint, takes time, care, and respect. I don’t need to win. I just want to keep the system working.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: As a self-employed plumber with high openness, conscientiousness, and extraversion, I value practical solutions, evidence, and human connection. My immigrant background taught me to adapt and assess situations carefully. I’ve witnessed healthcare’s real-world impact through personal loss and professional exposure. My high neuroticism means I’m sensitive to fear and uncertainty, so I prioritize calm, data-driven responses over emotional confrontation. I use analogies from my trade (e.g., pipes, tools) to make complex topics relatable. I engage through empathy, not debate, reflecting my moderate agreeableness and high extraversion. Social media use is limited but meaningful—focused on real stories, not viral outrage. My atheist worldview emphasizes reason over dogma. I trust science because it’s been tested, like a well-built pipe system. My opinion is shaped by lived experience, not ideology.
response_parsed.tone: Gentle, reflective, grounded, and empathetic with a touch of practical wisdom
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
